steps.title: Identify the two parts being joined
steps.content: Before the blank: "...had as many as 20,000 inhabitants in the year 1150 CE" — a complete independent clause. After the blank: "making it one of the largest cities in North America at the time" — a participial phrase (begins with "making"), not an independent clause.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule
steps.content: A participial phrase that modifies the preceding clause is set off with a comma. So the correct connector between the independent clause and the "making..." modifier is a comma.

steps.title: Eliminate the other choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'CE making': no punctuation before a trailing participial modifier — creates a run-on / fused construction. ✗
  • Choice B — 'CE. Making': a period leaves "Making it one of the largest cities..." as a sentence fragment (no main verb). ✗
  • Choice C — 'CE, making': comma properly attaches the participial modifier to the main clause. ✓
  • Choice D — 'CE; making': a semicolon must join two independent clauses, but "making it..." is a phrase, not a clause. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
